content:
I built an AI that’s supposed to learn from my codebase. I’m going to find out — in public — whether it actually does.

3 min read
Just now

--

Episode 0 of a series where I test my own claim instead of marketing it.
A little while ago I wrote that the bottleneck in AI coding isn’t a smarter model or more agents — it’s a feedback loop. Two halves: verification (the agent’s work has to pass your tests before it counts) and memory (it extracts a lesson from each task and feeds the relevant ones into the next one, so it stops repeating mistakes on your repo).
I stand by the verification half. It’s the part I’d defend.
The memory half, I ended that piece with an admission: I built the whole mechanism — lessons get extracted, stored, retrieved, injected — but I have no number showing it actually helps. It might help. It might just be adding confident-sounding noise to the prompt and burning tokens. Auto-extracted “lessons” from a language model skew toward platitudes (“make sure tests pass”), and I haven’t proven the sharp ones outweigh the junk.
So here’s what I’m not going to do: ship it, slap “it learns from your repo” on the README, and hope. That’s the claim every tool in this space makes and none of them measure.
Here’s what I’m going to do instead — in the open, whatever it shows.
The experiment
A fixed set of coding tasks, each with an acceptance contract (tests that must pass). I run the whole set twice:

• Memory off — the agent gets each task cold.

• Memory on — the agent gets the lessons it distilled from earlier tasks, retrieved and injected into the prompt.
Same model, same tasks, same everything. The only variable is whether it’s allowed to remember. Then I look at the boring numbers: first-try success rate, success rate after self-repair, and whether it repeats mistakes it already “learned” about.
If memory helps, there should be a gap. If there’s no gap, there’s no moat — and I’d rather know that now than build six more months on top of a comforting assumption.
What I honestly expect
I don’t know. Genuinely. My priors are split between “small real lift” and “basically noise.” There’s even a real chance it comes out negative — that injecting stale or generic lessons makes it worse. Whatever it is, I’ll post it. A disappointing result is a more useful post than another hype thread, and it’s the only version of this experiment worth doing.
What’s next
E1 will be how I’m actually designing the eval — the task set, how I measure “it learned,” how I keep it from cheating. I’d genuinely like to be argued with about the methodology before I start trusting the numbers, because a bad eval that flatters my own tool is worse than no eval at all.
If you want to watch me either validate or quietly demolish my own thesis, follow along. It’s early, and the outcome isn’t decided. That’s the point.
Next: E1 — designing an eval that can actually tell whether an AI learned your codebase (and can’t be gamed by the tool’s author).
